coredata.dc:title: Revealing the trap emission in graphene-based nanostructures
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shunwei
Zhao, Yanling
Ullah, Naeem
Wan, Qun
Zhang, Ruiqin
coredata.dc:description: Enhancing the photoconversion efficiency of graphene-based nanomaterials, especially graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), has been a long pursued interest in diverse optical applications. Though chemical modification has shown its effectiveness to enhance the photoluminescence efficiencies of these materials, an accurate, comprehensive fundamental understanding of this enhancement is demanded for further development. Herein, density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) calculations reveal that chemical species on the graphene nanostructure surface perturb the intrinsic electronic structures of the graphene moiety, with the perturbation manner and extent crucially depending on the binding positions of the species. In several configurations studied, the surface species lead to significantly enlarged oscillator strengths for the lowest optical transition (S1→S0) and localized excitons (bounded electron and hole) compared to those of the pristine nanographene structures, indicative of their role in enhancing the photoluminescence as emission centers. By contrast, the surface species in the other configurations show trivial influence on the emission efficiency, as indicated by the only slightly altered electronic/excitonic structures and oscillator strengths. These results provide a fundamental understanding of the trap emission mechanism and also the tuning/engineering of photoluminescence efficiencies in relevant materials.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Absorption and emission features of 7gnr and 96gqd examined by the TD-DFT CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d) level. The absorption spectrum was simulated by calculating the oscillator strengths (f) of 30 lowest excitations, with a half-width at half-height of 0.15 eV. The dominant excited states for the main absorption peaks are labeled along with each peak. The dotted vertical lines and the asterisks show the positions of the S0→S1 and S1→S0 transition, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Considered binding positions of the chemical groups on the 7gnr surface: one epoxy O atom bridging the two adjacent carbon atoms at positions 1–9 (the resulted structures are named as 7gnr-O-pn, n = 1–9), and two –OH/–H groups attaching to the two adjacent carbons at positions 1 and 5 (the resulted structures are named as 7gnr-2OH/H-pm, m = 1, 5). (b). Relative structural stability of each type of the 7gnr derivatives. The reference points are taken as the total electronic energies of the reactant 7gnr with one O atom, two OH or two H radicals, respectively. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Influence of chemical modification on the ground-state electronic structures of pristine 7gnr and its derivatives. (a) Energy levels of 20 highest occupied orbitals and 20 lowest virtual orbitals. The values in the middle show HOMO–LUMO gap energies. (b) HOMO–LUMO orbital distributions of pristine 7gnr and its representative derivatives (all HOMO–LUMO orbitals in this study are plotted with an isosurface value of 0.015 electron/Bohr3). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Natural transition orbitals (NTOs) corresponding to the S1→S0 transition of 7gnr and its representative derivatives at the S1 geometry. The left and right NTOs represent the spatial distributions of the electron and hole, respectively, when the electronic transition occurs. The percentage values are the corresponding weights of each NTO pair to describe the transition. All the NTOs in this study are plotted with an isosurface value of 0.015 electron/Bohr3. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
Proposed general roles of surface chemical species on the luminescence of GNRs and GQDs. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Influence of surface groups on the optical properties of 7gnr and its derivatives: the lowest excitation energy ( E
S0→S1), the emission energy ( E
S1→S0) and corresponding oscillator strength (f S1→S0) and main molecular orbitals involved in the S1→S0 transition, as well as the Stokes shift energy (ΔST) between the emission and the lowest excitation.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: H and L represent HOMO and LUMO orbitals, respectively. Only molecular orbital transitions with a weight larger than 15% are shown in the table.

head.ce:title: Revealing the trap emission in graphene-based nanostructures

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Department of Physics, City University of Hong Kong, SAR, Hong Kong, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Enhancing the photoconversion efficiency of graphene-based nanomaterials, especially graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), has been a long pursued interest in diverse optical applications. Though chemical modification has shown its effectiveness to enhance the photoluminescence efficiencies of these materials, an accurate, comprehensive fundamental understanding of this enhancement is demanded for further development. Herein, density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) calculations reveal that chemical species on the graphene nanostructure surface perturb the intrinsic electronic structures of the graphene moiety, with the perturbation manner and extent crucially depending on the binding positions of the species. In several configurations studied, the surface species lead to significantly enlarged oscillator strengths for the lowest optical transition (S1→S0) and localized excitons (bounded electron and hole) compared to those of the pristine nanographene structures, indicative of their role in enhancing the photoluminescence as emission centers. By contrast, the surface species in the other configurations show trivial influence on the emission efficiency, as indicated by the only slightly altered electronic/excitonic structures and oscillator strengths. These results provide a fundamental understanding of the trap emission mechanism and also the tuning/engineering of photoluminescence efficiencies in relevant materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene-related nanomaterials, particularly graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and graphene nanoribbons (GNRs), have shown great potential in a wide variety of optical applications [1,2]. For example, GQDs have achieved substantial development in optoelectronics, bioimaging, and sensoring [3–5]; for GNRs, it is of particular note that several very recent experiments have pioneered their development for potential high-tech optoelectronic applications, such as quantum information and quantum computing [1,6–8]. To overcome the limitations of the low photoconversion efficiencies in the pristine structures of GQDs and GNRs, developing materials with high photoluminescent intensity has attracted tremendous research interest for the purpose of their practical and effective use [1,6,9–11].

A large number of experimental approaches have been proposed to enhance the light emission efficiencies of these materials, such as chemically functionalizing their structures, tuning their compositions, inducing defects, and controlling their particle sizes [1,4,12]. Among these methods, chemical covalent modification of the graphene surface stands out for its ease of operation and the great tunability on the light emission region, but it suffers from the fact that the synthesized samples possess remarkably broad absorption and emission bands due to the large structural heterogeneity [3,9,13]. In this context, a detailed understanding of the structure–optical property relationships has become a hot research topic in order to experimentally achieve precise control over the optical features, including the photoconversion efficiencies. Thus far, enormous experimental and theoretical efforts have been expended with the aim of gaining deep insights into these relationships from the molecular level [14–17]. However, as one of the common origins of photoluminescence regularly suggested by experimentalists, light emission induced by the surface groups (trap emission) in these materials is still largely unknown, and several basic aspects, including the mechanism of trap emission, the chemical nature of the emission center, and the relationship between the trap emission and the emission energy, remain to be understood [6,9,12,18,19].

To provide a fundamental understanding, we theoretically investigate the influence of three kinds of common chemical groups (epoxy (–COC–), hydroxyl (–OH), and hydrogen (–H) groups) on the optoelectronic properties of two graphene-based nanostructures (namely, 7gnr and 96gqd), with a particular focus on their potential influence on the photoluminescent efficiencies. Density functional theory (DFT) and time-dependent DFT (TD-DFT) calculations reveal that the photoluminescent capabilities of graphene nanostructures shall drastically vary with the covalent binding positions of chemical groups on the graphene surfaces. In several of the studied structural configurations, the chemical groups localize (trap) the lowest exciton in their vicinity and in the meantime substantially enlarge the oscillator strength (f) for the S1→S0 transition compared to those of the pristine structures, showing the potential as emission traps to facilitate radiative decay. However, in the other configurations, the chemical groups only slightly deviate the magnitude of the oscillator strengths and the intrinsic electronic (excitonic) structures of the sp2 carbon moiety, indicative of their trivial influence on the emission efficiency. This study highlights the essential role of molecular structures on the optical selection rules in the related materials, and hence, the importance of precise chemical control over the structural configurations to achieving desirable optoelectronic properties.

The two graphene-based nanostructure models (that is, 7gnr and 96gqd) investigated in this study are shown in Fig. 1. Since GNRs are a kind of emerging luminescent materials for optoelectronic devices and a comprehensive understanding of their luminescence is especially needed by experiments [1,6,7], we mainly focused on the GNR system (7gnr) in our discussion (these two structures led to consistent conclusions: see below and also the supporting information). This pristine (unfunctionalized) GNR structure has a width of 7 atoms, contains 108 carbon atoms, has the edges terminated by 36 hydrogen atoms, and is approximately 3.5 nm in length and 0.9 nm in width. Many other types of GNRs have also been experimentally synthesized [6,20,21]. To study the influence of different surface groups on the optical properties, the absorption and emission features of the graphene nanostructures were investigated after chemically attaching the three common chemical species (that is, the –COC–, –OH, and –H groups) to the nanographene surface [3]. Note that the experimentally synthesized graphene nanostructures often possess large homogeneities in the size, shape, and compositions, but due to the limitation of the computational capabilities, we have only studied a set of structural models as representatives by choosing the types of graphene nanostructures as well as the types and the binding patterns of chemical groups.

All calculations were performed on the basis of DFT and its time-dependent extension, TD-DFT, as implemented in the Gaussian 09 package [22]. The range-separated CAM-B3LYP functional [23], arguably a superior hybrid for the study of excited states [24], together with the 6-31G(d) basis set [25], was chosen for the calculations. The accuracy of the adopted level of theory was justified by comparing results with another popularly used functional, ωB97XD [24,26], and also with previous theoretical and experimental studies (see Fig. S1 and below discussions). The absorption spectra were simulated according to the calculated oscillator strengths versus vertical excitation energies at the ground singlet (S0) state. The photon emission was assumed to occur from the electronically excited singlet (S1) state, following the Kasha's rule, as mostly revealed for the graphene-related nanostructures, and the emission energy was calculated at the minimum energy point of the structures after conducting excited-state structural optimization [18,27,28].

The absorption and emission features of 7gnr and 96gqd as revealed with the CAM-B3LYP/6-31G(d) level of theory, which shows the physical structure dependent optical properties of nanographenes, are summarized in Fig. 1
[14]. Since the absorption and emission properties of pristine nanographenes have been extensively investigated in both experimental and theoretical studies [6,29], we only briefly discuss our results here. As shown, among the lowest 30 excitations considered, 7gnr has the largest oscillator strength value for the S0→S7 transition at approximately 360 nm, whereas 96gqd shows the largest oscillator strength values for the degenerate S3 and S4 states at approximately 445 nm (see Table S1 for detailed information of the 30 excitations). Most importantly, the first excitations (S0→S1) of both 7gnr and 96gqd have negligible oscillator strengths (0.004 and 0.000, respectively), indicating the symmetry-forbidden nature of these transitions (due to the different structural symmetry, the optical transitions of these two structures show a slight difference in the oscillator strength) [28]. Such symmetry-related optical excitation in pristine graphene-based nanostructures has been in detail analyzed based on the transition-dipole analysis in previous studies [30,31]. After structural relaxation in the S1 state, the oscillator strength values for the S1→S0 transitions in these two structures remain to be about zero, implying the very low radiative decay probability for the S1 states. Analogous to the case of single-wall carbon nanotubes (SWCNTs), the general low emission efficiencies of pristine GNRs and GQDs has been importantly ascribed to the forbidden nature of the lowest S1 state [6,29,32,33]. Binding chemical groups onto nanographene surface changes the optical features of the graphene nanostructures, including the probability of the lowest transition, as shown by the absorption spectra of the 7gnr derivatives (Figs. S2 and S3). The results here support the hypothesis that enhancing the photoluminescent efficiencies can be achieved by activating the lowest-energy ‘dark’ state [6,29,34].

The –COC–, –OH, and –H groups were chosen to carry out these studies since they are common species in graphene-based materials [4,35]. Owing to the high computational cost, only a set of configurational structures were considered (Fig. 2a): nine unique positions (positions 1–9) on the 7gnr basal plane were selected to chemically bind one epoxy group; subsequent calculations were only performed for the configurations with two –OH or –H groups bound to positions 1 and 5, which are the most and least stable binding positions for the epoxy oxygen atom, respectively (to maintain the structure with no unpaired electron, two –OH/–H groups were attached to the surface). We also examined two representative isomers of 96gqd epoxide and obtained a consistent conclusion; however, for the simplicity of discussion, we here mainly present the results of the 7gnr system (see discussions below and Table S2 as well as Fig. S4 for the results of the 96gqd system).

Prior to inspecting the electronic and optical properties of 7gnr and its derivatives, the relative structural stabilities of the 7gnr derivatives were compared by computing the energy needed or released to covalently bind one epoxy O atom, two –OH or two –H groups onto the 7gnr surface (ΔE B = E 7gnr derivative − E 7gnr – E Chemical group). As depicted in Fig. 2b, the computed binding energies in each kind of structure notably vary relative to a reference point, which is taken as the total electronic energy of the corresponding reactants, but all the configurational structures are energetically favorable. Specifically, the binding energies of the epoxy O in the nine 7gnr mono-epoxides range from 30.1 to 69.1 kcal/mol, the two 7gnr hydroxides show a binding energy difference of 75.2 kcal/mol, and the two 7gnr hydrides have a binding energy difference of 63.8 kcal/mol. These results indicate the pronounced influence of the configuration on the structural stabilities, as reported previously [36,37]. The difference in the binding energies of the structural configurations is related to the degree of disruption of the sp2 carbon network upon attaching the chemical groups onto the nanographene surface and to the resulting changes in the electronic structures of the nanographenes. It is predictable that the attached chemical groups may also affect the optical transitions, as shown below. Specifically, attaching chemical groups onto the edge carbon atoms of the nanographenes leads to slight perturbation to the intrinsic electronic structures and therefore slightly changes the optical transitions; by contrast, the attachment of functional groups to the center carbon atoms may effectively tune the optical properties.

As a crucial factor determining the optical behaviors, the electronic structures of 7gnr and its derivatives with sp3 defects are inspected at their ground state geometries. As depicted in Fig. 3 a, 7gnr has an electronic gap of 4.529 eV; after binding the chemical species, the electronic gaps of the resulting derivatives vary from 3.889 to 4.845 eV. Such a large gap variation results from the molecular orbital hybridization between 7gnr and the chemical groups, which is reflected in the spatial redistributions of the HOMO–LUMO orbitals, as exemplified in Fig. 3b (see HOMO–LUMO distributions of the other structures in Fig. S5) [38]. As shown, the HOMO–LUMO orbitals of 7gnr are delocalized over the sp2 carbon backbone; the changes in the HOMO–LUMO distributions of the derivatives compared to that of 7gnr can be classified into two types. First, significant localization of the orbitals around and on the epoxy O atom, as shown in the case of the representative 7gnr-O-p1 and 7gnr-2OH-p1. Second, redistributed HOMO-LUMO orbitals but essentially retain their delocalized nature, as seen from the orbitals of 7gnr-O-p5 and 7gnr-2OH-p5. In the first case, the decrease in the HOMO-LUMO gap is related to the emergence of defect state, whereas in the second case the HOMO-LUMO gap is primarily determined by the quantum confinement effect [31,39], that is, the size of the spatial distribution of the orbitals is negatively correlated with the HOMO-LUMO gap value. The perturbation in the electronic structures may lead to alteration of the optical selection rules, for instance, it may convert the optically forbidden transitions into allowed transitions [28,33,40]. In fact, this is what we found and discussed in the following. Compared to another manner of covalent functionalization to the nanographenes, that is, terminating the nanographene edges with chemical groups while preserving the sp2 hybridization nature of the carbon atoms, the functionalization manner studied in this work (altering the sp2 carbon to sp3 carbon) is likely to induce more deviation to the optical properties of the nanographenes as it greatly perturbs the intrinsic electronic structures of the carbon moiety. However, the sp2-preserving edge functionalization manner may still effectively change the optical properties, for example, when the nanographene edges are functionalized with a large number of specific chemical groups which can notably distort the nanographene backbones, as revealed previously [17,41].

Previous studies revealed that both the absorption and emission regions of the graphene nanosheets are critically dependent on the binding positions of chemical groups on the sp2 carbon lattice [37,42]. An analogous result is found here, as indicated by the lowest excitation and emission energies (ranging from 2.18 to 2.98 eV and from 1.68 to 2.81 eV, respectively) of the 7gnr epoxides shown in Table 1 . These results indicate that in experiments, defects at different positions on the nanographene surface may lead to multiple (broaden) emission peaks instead of a peak with specific photon energy, highlighting the importance of structural homogeneity to the homogeneity in the emitted light. Furthermore, as indicated by the noticeably different magnitudes of the oscillator strengths of the S1→S0 transition, these configurational structures may exhibit distinct light emission capabilities [28,33]. Specifically, the oscillator strength for the pristine 7gnr is merely 0.004, indicating a negligible radiative transition probability and thus a poor light emission ability, in agreement with the experimental observations that pristine GNRs are either nonemissive or have low emission intensities [43]. After anchoring the chemical species onto the 7gnr backbone, some of the 7gnr derivatives (7gnr-O-pn (n ≥ 4), 7gnr-2OH-p5, and 7gnr-2H-p5) possess similar oscillator strength magnitudes compared to that of 7gnr; however, the other structures (i.e., 7gnr-O-p1, 7gnr-O-p2, 7gnr-O-p3, 7gnr-2OH-p1, and 7gnr-2H-p1) feature significantly enlarged oscillator strength values, which are two-to-three fold greater than the value of 7gnr. A similar result is also observed for the 96gqd system, which shows an even larger change in the oscillator strengths (from 0.000 to 0.2202), as summarized in Table S2. The appearance of such notably increased oscillator strengths results from the electronic structure deviation, implying the role of surface defects in elevating the radiative transition probability by converting the forbidden or weakly allowed transitions into strongly allowed transitions, analogous to the mechanism reported for SWCNTs [44–47]. In addition, these results suggest that the role of chemical groups in increasing the oscillator strengths of the S1→S0 transition is crucially affected by the associated functionalization pattern, whereas the chemical nature of the groups may play a less pivotal role.

Table 1 also shows the Stokes shift energies (ΔST) between the emission and first absorption levels of the investigated structures. The extent of energy loss of the emission is associated with vibrational reorganization due to structural deformation upon photoexcitation [48,49], corresponding to the magnitude of changes in the HOMO-LUMO gaps and in the orbital distributions [50]. As shown, 7gnr-O-p1, 7gnr-O-p2, 7gnr-O-p3, 7gnr-2OH-p1, and 7gnr-2H-p1 possess much larger ST energies than the other configurations (for example, 3–5 folds of the value for 7gnr), resulted from a much more prominent deformation of their geometries due to exciton trapping at the defect sites. By contrast, the other structural configurations show less significant changes in the electronic and excitonic structures relative to those of the pristine structure. As an indicator of trapping strength, the much larger ST values correspond to a deeper trapping potential well [50,51], which enables the effective localization (pinning) of the mobile excitons to the surface defects, whereas the smaller values indicate a shallow trapping well and thereby a limited influence on the delocalization of excitons [51]. It should be noted that there are conflicting views on the origin of the redshifted emission in the graphene-based nanomaterials after photoexcitation [52], herein we emphasize that surface chemical groups are likely to be the primary determinants of both the small and the large emission energy losses [53].

The emission trap role of the chemical species can be interpreted from the distributions of the electron and hole at the energy minima geometry of the S1 state [32,40]. To do so, natural transition orbitals (NTOs) corresponding to the S1→S0 transition are plotted. As summarized in Table 1, the S1→S0 electronic transitions in several of the studied structures involve multiple pairs of molecular orbitals; for example, the two main contributions (H→L and H-1→L+1) in 7gnr-2OH-p5 only account for ∼54% of the transition. Therefore, NTOs are utilized since NTOs allow for a dramatic simplification of the description of electronic transitions involving multiple molecular orbitals [54]. As shown in Fig. 4 , one or two pairs of NTOs can account for over 80% of the S1→S0 transition; this ensures that the NTOs can satisfactorily represent the distributions of the electron and hole (see also the electron-hole distributions of the other structures in Fig. S6). All the S1→S0 transitions are characterized by electron transition between π* and π molecular orbitals (π*→π transition). For 7gnr, 7gnr-O-p1, and 7gnr-2OH-p1, the electron/hole NTOs in the S1 state are essentially delocalized across the system. In sharp contrast, the attached chemical species in 7gnr-O-p1 and 7gnr-2OH-p1 lead to markedly localized electron and hole in the vicinity of the sp3 defect as well as a considerable electron density population on the attached species; these results are compelling evidence for exciton trapping in the S1 states of these two structures. It can be expected that a species that can induce localized excitons may trap the initially mobile electron and hole after excitation and facilitate their recombination to emit a photon by acting as emission centers [33,44].

Last, we generalize our findings to the broad family of GNRs and GQDs, as depicted in Fig. 5 . After excitation, the non-radiative decay of the excited states brings the excited electrons to the lowest S1 state, in which the recombination of the electron and hole is affected by the surface-bound chemical species. Depending on the binding position, the defects on the graphene surface can either effectively localize the electron and hole or just slightly perturb their diffusion. In the former case, the surface defects may great improve the luminescent intensities of the materials by localizing the lowest excitons and remarkably enlarging the oscillator strengths of the S1→S0 transition, whereas in the latter, the surface defects shall impart a negligible influence on the S1→S0 transition probability compared to that of the pristine structures. It is noteworthy that a phenomenon in which the surface groups increase the photoluminescent efficiencies by acting as emission traps has been elucidated for SWCNTs [33,40,44,51,55,56]. The development of GNRs and GQDs for high-tech optoelectronic applications remains in the very early stages compared to that of SWCNTs [1,55]. In this context, previous studies on SWCNTs will prove to be a valuable reference for the research of GNRs and other related materials.

By performing DFT and TD-DFT calculations, this study reveals the influence of surface chemical groups (–COC–, –OH, and –H groups) on the optoelectronic properties of graphene nanostructures. In addition to the absorption and emission energies, the photoluminescent efficiencies of the examined structures are predicted to strongly depend on the binding positions of the chemical species on the graphene surface, with the chemical nature of the species playing a less prominent role. Depending on the binding positions, the surface species may lead to significantly enlarged oscillator strength of the lowest transition (S1→S0) and notably localized exciton compared to that of the pristine nanographene structures, signifying their role in enhancing the photoluminescence by acting as emission centers. Such a structural dependence of optical behaviors is associated with modifications of the intrinsic electronic structures of the sp2 carbon moiety upon attachment of the chemical species. This study provides a detailed understanding of the trap emission mechanism and offers guidance to tune/engineer the exciton emission in relevant graphene-based materials.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by grants from the Research Grants Council of the Hong Kong SAR [Project No. 11305618], the Innovation Committee of Shenzhen Municipality (JCYJ20170818104105891), and the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21703190). We acknowledge the High Performance Cluster Computing Center in Hong Kong Baptist University, which receives funding from the Research Grant Council, the University Grant Committee of the HKSAR, and Hong Kong Baptist University, for providing the computational resources.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable room-temperature single-photon emission at telecom wavelengths from sp3 defects in carbon nanotubes
